name: pitfalls-security
description: Security patterns for session keys, caching, logging, and environment variables. Use when implementing authentication, caching sensitive data, or setting up logging. Triggers on: session key, private key, cache, logging, secrets, environment variable.
capabilities: Guides secure session key implementation with expiry and permission limits
Shows correct environment variable handling for frontend and backend
Demonstrates safe caching strategies for non-sensitive data
Provides structured logging patterns that avoid secret exposure
Includes audit logging templates for sensitive operations
Offers checklists for verifying security implementations
author: BarisSozen
securitySummary: This is a pure documentation skill containing only markdown guidance. No executable code, scripts, network calls, or filesystem access capabilities. The TypeScript examples are educational documentation showing security patterns (anti-patterns marked with ❌, correct patterns marked with ✅). All static findings are false positives triggered by pattern matching on educational examples rather than actual vulnerabilities.
valueStatement: Security vulnerabilities in authentication, caching, and logging expose sensitive user data and credentials. This skill provides verified patterns to prevent common mistakes when handling session keys, environment variables, and sensitive data.
limitations: Does not provide runtime security scanning of existing code
Does not generate security credentials or encryption keys
Does not integrate with external security scanning tools
Does not provide legal compliance verification

useCases.title: Secure session management
useCases.description: Implement session keys with proper expiry, permissions, and audit logging for user authentication

useCases.title: Environment variable safety
useCases.description: Configure environment variables correctly to prevent secret exposure in frontend or backend code

useCases.title: Review security implementations
useCases.description: Verify code follows security best practices for caching, logging, and credential handling

promptTemplates.title: Session key setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Use the pitfalls-security skill to help me implement secure session key management with proper expiration and permission limits

promptTemplates.title: Environment variables
promptTemplates.prompt: Apply pitfalls-security patterns to set up environment variables for my backend API without exposing secrets in logs

promptTemplates.title: Caching strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: Use pitfalls-security to design a caching strategy that avoids storing any user credentials or sensitive data

promptTemplates.title: Audit logging
promptTemplates.prompt: Apply pitfalls-security to create an audit logging system that captures security-sensitive operations without logging secrets

bestPractices: Always use encryption like AES-256-GCM when storing any credentials
Implement proper session key expiry and permission limits
Add audit logging for all sensitive operations with before/after states
antiPatterns: Storing private keys in localStorage or plaintext files
Logging entire config objects that may contain secrets
Caching user-specific sensitive data like private keys or API tokens

faq.question: Is this skill compatible with all Claude versions?
faq.answer: Yes, the security patterns work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code across all current versions.

faq.question: What data can I safely cache?
faq.answer: Only non-sensitive data like public token prices, pool reserves, and gas estimates. Never cache credentials or private keys.

faq.question: How do I handle frontend environment variables?
faq.answer: Use VITE_ prefix for Vite frontend builds. Remember process.env does not work in browser contexts.

faq.question: Is my data safe when using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, this skill only provides guidance patterns. No data is collected, stored, or transmitted externally.

faq.question: Why are my secrets appearing in logs?
faq.answer: Avoid logging entire config objects. Use structured logging with explicit field filtering to exclude sensitive values.

faq.question: How does this compare to standard security linters?
faq.answer: This skill provides implementation patterns. Combine it with linters like ESLint security plugins for automated scanning.
